title: Gates and Boolean Algebra
summary: Understand the basic gates used to build digital circuits and how Boolean operators describe their behavior.
content_html:
Digital circuits are built from logic gates. A gate receives one or more binary inputs and produces a binary output. In Boolean algebra, 1 usually means true or high voltage, while 0 means false or low voltage.

The main gates are NOT, AND, OR, XOR, NAND, NOR, and XNOR. NOT inverts one input. AND outputs 1 only when all required inputs are 1. OR outputs 1 when at least one input is 1. XOR outputs 1 when the two inputs are different.

NOT:  A' or !A
AND:  A . B or A & B
OR:   A + B or A | B
XOR:  A xor B or A ^ B
NAND: (A . B)'
NOR:  (A + B)'

NAND is especially important because any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates. This makes it a universal gate for digital design.

exercises.title: AND gate behavior
exercises.prompt: When does a 2-input AND gate output 1?

exercises.title: Universal gate
exercises.prompt: Which gate can be used by itself to construct any Boolean function?

exercises.title: NOT notation
exercises.prompt: Write one common Boolean algebra notation for NOT A.

exercises.title: XOR meaning
exercises.prompt: A 2-input XOR gate outputs 1 when the two inputs are what?
